title: China AI Price War Deepens, G7 Grapples With AI Sovereignty, and Beijing Regulates AI Agents
subhead: China's AI firms slash prices to near-zero as ByteDance and Xiaomi ignite a race to the bottom, the G7 debates who gets access to cutting-edge US AI models, Beijing moves to rein in humanlike AI agents, and the UK bans social media for under-16s — a day of divergent global tech governance.

sections.heading: China's AI Price War Spirals as Models Go Nearly Free
sections.body:
China's AI sector is in a full-throttle price war. ByteDance launched Seedance 2.0 Mini at 23 yuan (US$3.40) per million tokens — half the price of its standard version — while Xiaomi cut MiMo V2.5 by 99 per cent, and Tencent slashed Hy-MT2-Pro prices nearly 70 per cent, according to SCMP. MiniMax halved pricing on its new M3 series, and Alibaba offered 50 per cent off Qwen3.7-Max for the 618 sales event.

Bank of America Securities noted that capability gaps between Chinese AI models are narrowing rapidly, forcing firms to compete on price rather than differentiation. The result: access to capable AI is becoming commoditized in China far faster than in Western markets, with potential implications for adoption velocity and global competitiveness.

Meanwhile, a widening valuation gap has opened between Chinese AI startups. Zhipu AI is gaining ground while MiniMax stumbles, according to Nikkei Asia, suggesting the price war is starting to thin the field even as it expands access.
sections.whyItMatters: China's AI models are racing to near-zero pricing while capabilities converge — this compresses margins to unsustainable levels for all but the most capitalized players, but could accelerate China's AI deployment velocity far ahead of regulated Western markets.

sections.heading: G7 Debates Trusted Partner Access to Cutting-Edge US AI Models
sections.body:
G7 leaders at the Evian summit discussed a plan to grant select trusted partners access to advanced AI models from US firms such as Anthropic, three diplomatic sources told Reuters. The proposal would create a framework for allies to use the most capable American AI systems while keeping them out of adversary hands.

The discussion comes amid the Trump administration's forced shutdown of Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on national security grounds, reported by The Hill. The order drew criticism that US AI regulation remains ad hoc — reacting to individual model releases rather than following a structured governance framework. A Channel News Asia commentary framed the intervention as a shift from Washington's prior hands-off posture to active model-level control.

The G7 conversation highlights a central tension: the US wants to both restrict frontier AI from adversaries and extend controlled access to allies, but lacks a consistent regulatory architecture to do either systematically.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is simultaneously pulling its own most advanced models off the shelf and proposing to share others with allies — the incoherence reveals a governance gap that adversarial nations are already exploiting.

sections.heading: Beijing Moves to Restrict Humanlike AI Agents as ByteDance and Alibaba Comply
sections.body:
ByteDance's Doubao and Alibaba's Qwen have disabled customized humanlike AI agent features as new Beijing regulations loom, according to SCMP. The move signals that Chinese authorities are extending their AI governance beyond model training and into deployment behavior — specifically targeting AI systems that mimic human interaction patterns.

The regulation arrives at a moment when Chinese AI firms are already squeezed by the domestic price war. Having to strip product features to comply with new rules adds another layer of competitive constraint — though it also signals Beijing's willingness to shape AI behavior in ways Western governments have been slower to attempt.
sections.whyItMatters: China is regulating AI agent behavior in real time, forcing platforms to disable features before rules even take effect — a pre-emptive governance approach that contrasts sharply with the reactive US posture.

sections.heading: UK Bans Social Media for Under-16s as Platform Regulation Accelerates Globally
sections.body:
Prime Minister Keir Starmer announced a ban on social media for children under 16, covering apps including TikTok, Snapchat, and YouTube, with restrictions expected to take effect in early 2027, according to Reuters. The legislation includes mandatory age verification and may impose overnight curfews on platform access, Ars Technica reported.

The UK move is among the most aggressive platform regulation globally and adds to a growing patchwork: Australia passed a similar under-16 ban in late 2024, the EU is tightening its Digital Services Act enforcement, and China has had minor time limits since 2021. The UK version goes further by including YouTube and potentially restricting access hours entirely for teenagers.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK is setting a new Western benchmark for platform control over minors — the inclusion of YouTube and overnight curfews goes beyond what any other democracy has attempted, and implementation will be watched closely as a test case.

sections.heading: RISC-V Cooperation Holds Despite US-China Rivalry, Alibaba Executive Says
sections.body:
Global cooperation on the RISC-V open-standard chip architecture remains resilient despite US-China technological rivalry, according to Alibaba vice-president Qi Xiaoning. Speaking at the SCMP China Conference 2026, Qi said major companies from the US, China, India, Brazil, and Europe are working together 'as one unified ecosystem' on RISC-V for edge and cloud applications.

The architecture has surged in popularity in China because it is free to use and modify, as Beijing races to reduce reliance on proprietary Western chip designs amid tightening export controls. But Qi's message was pointedly collaborative: 'When we use a single architecture, people from different continents, different cultural backgrounds, we get together, we work together and make friends.'

The statement is significant because RISC-V has been identified by US hawks as a potential sanctions-evasion vector. If Washington moves to restrict RISC-V participation, the split would fracture one of the few remaining areas of genuine global semiconductor cooperation.
sections.whyItMatters: RISC-V is the last open door in semiconductor sovereignty — a space where Chinese, American, and Indian engineers still share standards. Pressure to close it would sever one of the few remaining bridges in chip technology.

sections.heading: China's Stealth Coating Breakthrough and the Democratization of Military Tech
sections.body:
A team from Foshan University reported a low-cost radar-absorbing composite made from expanded graphite and titanium dioxide, according to a peer-reviewed paper in the Journal of Ceramics reported by SCMP. The material could make stealth coating dramatically cheaper — replacing expensive ingredients like graphene and carbon nanotubes with readily available industrial materials.

Separately, Morgan Stanley forecast the global robotaxi market reaching US$1 trillion by 2040, driven by falling manufacturing costs in China. The bank projects Chinese-made robotaxi hardware costs dropping to US$35,000–40,000 per vehicle by 2027, with services covering operating costs by 2028 and scaled commercial operations by 2030. Chinese players Baidu, Xpeng, and WeRide are positioned as regional front-runners alongside Tesla and Waymo.

Both developments reflect a broader pattern: China is systematizing the ability to turn advanced technology into mass-produced, low-cost products — whether the product is stealth coating or autonomous vehicles.
sections.whyItMatters: The pattern is consistent across domains — China is compressing the gap between prototype and mass production, turning specialized military and dual-use technologies into commodities.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Plan Faces Skepticism as Takaichi Pushes Stockpiling
sections.body:
The Trump administration's push for a Western critical minerals pricing bloc faces skepticism from G7 partners and divisions within the US mining industry over costs and a Pentagon AI pricing system, Reuters reported. The proposal aims to counter China's dominance over rare earths and battery mineral supply chains but has run into concerns about feasibility and market distortion.

Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi, at her G7 debut, proposed a joint strategic stockpile partnership to bolster critical mineral supply chains and reduce Chinese dominance, according to SCMP. Takaichi positioned herself as a bridge between Trump and skeptical European leaders while promoting Japan's interest in supply chain resilience.

Meanwhile, Singapore's non-oil domestic exports surged 38.4 per cent year-on-year in May, driven by AI-related demand, according to Nikkei Asia — a signal that despite geopolitical tensions, the AI supply chain continues to generate massive trade flows through Southeast Asia.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 wants to break China's minerals grip but can't agree on how — Japan's stockpiling proposal is the most concrete alternative so far, while Singapore's export surge shows the AI trade is still flowing through channels the West can't easily reroute.

bottomLine: China's AI firms are racing prices to the floor while Beijing tightens control over how those models behave — the simultaneous commoditization and regulation of Chinese AI creates a distinctive governance model the rest of the world hasn't matched. At the G7, the US wants to both pull its most advanced models and share others with allies, but has no framework for either. The UK is setting a new Western benchmark for platform control by banning social media for under-16s. And from RISC-V chips to stealth coatings to robotaxis, China keeps demonstrating the same capability: turning advanced technology into cheap, mass-produced products faster than anyone expected.
sources: SCMP, Reuters, Nikkei Asia, The Hill, Channel News Asia, Ars Technica, Morgan Stanley, Bank of America Securities, Foshan University (Journal of Ceramics), Defense News, AP News
